title: Global regularity for a slightly supercritical hyperdissipative Navier-Stokes system

thm:1.1.summary: Global smooth solutions for the dyadic shell model (eq:1.1) under the optimal dissipation condition $\intds/sG(s) = \infty$. This is the shell-model version of the main result.

thm:1.2.summary: Global smooth solutions for the generalized hyperdissipative NS (eq:1.6) when $\alpha+\beta \ge (d+2)/2$ and $\intds/sg(s) = \infty$. Proves Tao's 2009 conjecture on the optimal regularity threshold.

def:2.2.summary: Shell approximation: $X_n(t) = (\Sigma \psi_n(k)|v_k(t)|^2)^{1/2}$ aggregates Fourier modes into dyadic shells. The key dimensional reduction from PDE to ODE system.

thm:2.3.summary: Bounded shell approximation implies global existence: if sup $\Sigma 2^{2mn} X_n^2 < \infty$ on [0,T_*), then $T_* = \infty$. Reduces the PDE problem to bounding the shell sequence.

def:2.4.summary: Shell solution: abstract ODE system $dX_n^2/dt = -\chi_n X_n^2 + \Sigma \varphi_{(l,m,n)} X_l X_m X_n$ with antisymmetric nonlinearity and dissipation/interaction bounds (eq:2.6). The framework for the entire proof.

rem:2.7.summary: Antisymmetry of $\varphi$ makes the nonlinear term formally energy-conservative (sum vanishes). This structural property is the mechanism behind lem:3.4's cancellation.

thm:2.8.summary: The shell approximation of any NS solution is a shell solution. Bridges the PDE and the abstract ODE framework, enabling the cascade argument.

prop:2.10.summary: Lower bound $\chi_n(t) \ge 2^{\alphan-\alpha+1}/g(2^{n+1})$ for the dissipation coefficient. Uses support properties of $\psi_n$ and monotonicity of g.

prop:2.11.summary: Properties of the interaction coefficient $\varphi$: vanishes outside the index set I (locality), satisfies antisymmetry $\varphi_{(l,m,n)} = -\varphi_{(l,n,m)}$, and obeys $|\varphi| \le$ c₃ $2^{(d/2+1-\beta)min{l,m,n}} (decay$ bound).

lem:2.12.summary: Symmetry identity for the interaction kernel: swapping $\psi_m$ and $\psi_n$ arguments negates the sum. Proved by the change of variable k' = h-k and using divergence-free + reality conditions.

lem:2.13.summary: Trilinear Cauchy-Schwarz bound: $\Sigma \psi_a(h)|v_h| \Sigma \sqrt(\psi_b \psi_c)|v_k||v_{k-h}| \le 2^{d(a+3)/2} X_a X_b X_c$. Converts pointwise products to shell norms.

def:3.1.summary: Energy inequality: $\Sigma X_n^2(t) + \int$₀ᵗ $\Sigma \chi_n X_n^2$ ds $\le \Sigma X_n^2(0)$. Expresses dissipation of total energy in the shell system.

def:3.2.summary: Tail $F_n(t) = \Sigma_{k\gen} X_k^2(t)$ and energy bound $d_n(t) = (F_n + \int \Sigma_{h\gen} \chi_h X_h^2$ ds)^{1/2}. The $d_n$ sequence is what the cascade argument drives to zero.

prop:3.3.summary: THE recursive inequality: $d_n^2 \le F_n(0) +$ c₄ $\Sigma_{l<n} (d$̄$_l/\lambda^{n-l}) \Sigma_{m\gen-2} g(2^{m+1})/\lambda^{m-n} (d_m^2 - d_{m+1}^2)$. Converts energy inequality + antisymmetric cancellation into a recursion solvable by the cascade.

lem:3.4.summary: Antisymmetric sum cancellation: $\Sigma_{h\len-1} \varphi X_l X_m X_h = -\Sigma_{m<n\leh} \varphi X_l X_m X_h$. Transfers energy flux from low shells to high shells, enabling the tail estimate in prop:3.3.

thm:4.1.summary: If a shell solution satisfies the energy inequality and has smooth initial data $(sup_n 2^{mn}|X_n(0)| < \infty$ for all m), then sup over time of the same quantity remains finite. The main technical result: smoothness is preserved.

lem:4.2.summary: Five properties of the cascade sequences $R_n, Q_n$: (1) min over an interval $\le d^2/\Sigmab$ via pigeonhole, (2) liminf $R_n = 0, (3)$ d̄_n ↓ 0, $(4) Q_n \to 0, (5) Q_n$ eventually non-increasing. Sets up the cascade induction.

lem:4.3.summary: Initial step of the cascade: for any M > 0, there exist n₀ and $\theta > 0$ with $Q_{n_k} \le \lambda^{-k/2}$ and d̄$_{n_k}^2 \le \lambda^{-Mk}$ along a subsequence. Launches the geometric decay.

lem:4.4.summary: For infinitely many k, $n_{k+1} = n_k + 1 (the$ cascade takes unit steps). Proof by contradiction: if steps are $\ge 2$ eventually, the series $\Sigma b_{n_k}$ both converges and diverges.

lem:4.5.summary: Full cascade recursion: d̄$_n^2 \le c_M \lambda^{-Mn}$ and $Q_n \le c_M \lambda^{-n}$ for every M > 0. Bootstraps the subsequence decay from lem:4.3 to every index via induction, using unit-step density from lem:4.4.

thm:A.1.summary: Local existence and uniqueness for eq:1.6 in $V_m (m \ge 2+d/2)$, with blowup criterion: either $T_* = \infty$ or limsup ‖v(t)‖_m $= \infty$. Standard but necessary for the global argument.

prop:A.2.summary: Quantitative local existence: solution exists on [0, T] with T < c_*/‖v₀‖_m, with a priori bound ‖$v_\varepsilon$‖_m $\le$ ‖v₀‖_m/(1 - c_* T ‖v₀‖_m). The bootstrap estimate.

lem:A.3.summary: Right-continuity of the solution in $V_m (strong$ topology) and of dv/dt in $V_{m-\alpha}$. Uses the a priori bound from prop:A.2 and weak continuity.

prop:A.4.summary: Blowup criterion: if $T_* < \infty$ then limsup ‖v(t)‖_m $= \infty$. Proved by contradiction — bounded norm lets you restart from a late time via prop:A.2, extending past T_*.

edges.note: Thm 1.1 follows from applying Thm 4.1 to the dyadic model (eq:1.1) viewed as a shell solution.
The dyadic model equation (eq:1.1) is a shell solution in the sense of Def 2.4.
Reduces NS global existence to showing the shell approximation stays bounded in $H^m$.
Needs that the NS solution's shell approximation is a shell solution to apply the cascade argument.
Thm 4.1 proves the shell solution stays smooth, giving the boundedness required by Thm 2.3.
Local existence (Thm A.1) provides the initial solution on [0,T_*) to be extended globally.
Uses the blowup criterion: if shell approximation is bounded but $T_* < \infty$, contradiction with limsup $= \infty$.
Defines the dissipation coefficient $\chi_n$ via Prop 2.10 and verifies the lower bound (eq:2.6).
Defines the interaction coefficient $\varphi$ via Prop 2.11 and verifies antisymmetry, locality, and bound (eq:2.6).
Shell approximation formula (eq:2.2) is used throughout the derivation of the shell solution ODE.
Proof verifies that X satisfies all properties in Def 2.4 (ODE structure, antisymmetry, coefficient bounds).
The $\chi_n$ lower bound uses the support of $\psi_n$ and the shell approximation formula (eq:2.2).
Antisymmetry $\varphi_{(l,m,n)} = -\varphi_{(l,n,m)}$ follows immediately from Lemma 2.12's kernel identity.
The bound $|\varphi| \le$ c₃ $2^{(d/2+1-\beta)min}$ in both Case 1 and Case 2 uses Lemma 2.13's trilinear estimate.
Uses the shell solution ODE (eq:2.5) and coefficient bounds (eq:2.6) from the shell solution definition.
Applies Lemma 3.4 to rewrite the sum over low shells as a sum over cross-boundary terms.
The energy inequality (3.1) bounds the integrated dissipation, enabling the tail estimate.
The entire inequality is expressed in terms of the tail $F_n$ and energy bound $d_n$ from Def 3.2.
Uses absolute convergence from the coefficient bound (eq:2.6) and antisymmetry of $\varphi$ from Def 2.4.
Exploits the antisymmetric cancellation mechanism described in Remark 2.7 (change of variable m'=n, n'=m).
The recursive inequality (3.2) is the foundation for defining $R_n, Q_n$ and running the cascade.
Lemma 4.5 gives d̄$_n^2 \le c_M \lambda^{-Mn}$ for every M, which is exactly the smoothness conclusion.
Properties of $R_n$ and $Q_n (convergence$, monotonicity) structure the entire cascade argument.
Uses the initial cascade step (subsequence with geometric decay) as the base case for induction.
The density of unit steps $(n_{k+1}=n_k+1$ i.o.) provides the $b_{n_k-1}$ lower bound needed to close the recursion.
Uses $Q_n \to 0$, d̄_n $\to 0$, and $Q_n$ eventually non-increasing (parts 3-5 of Lem 4.2) to choose n₀.
The pigeonhole bound (4.2) and convergence properties are derived from the recursive inequality (3.2).
Local existence and the a priori bound come from Prop A.2.
Right-continuity in $V_m (strong$ topology) from Lemma A.3.
Blowup criterion $(T_* < \infty$ ⟹ limsup $= \infty)$ from Prop A.4.
Uses the a priori bound (A.2) to show limsup ‖v(t)‖_m $\le$ ‖v₀‖_m at t=0, then weak-strong argument.
Contradiction: if bounded and $T_* < \infty$, Prop A.2 restarts from T₀ = T_* - c_*/(4M) and extends past T_*.

main_results.description: The two main theorems: global regularity for the dyadic shell model (Thm 1.1) and for generalized hyperdissipative NS (Thm 1.2, proving Tao's 2009 conjecture)

shell_model_framework.description: Dimensional reduction from PDE to abstract ODE system. Shell approximation aggregates Fourier modes into dyadic shells; Thm 2.3 reduces global existence to bounding the shell sequence; Thm 2.8 verifies NS solutions produce shell solutions

coefficient_estimates.description: Quantitative properties of the dissipation $(\chi)$ and interaction $(\varphi)$ coefficients: lower bounds, antisymmetry, locality, and decay. These feed into the recursive inequality

recursive_inequality.description: The core mechanism: energy inequality + antisymmetric cancellation yield a recursive bound on the energy tails $d_n$. Prop 3.3 is the engine of the entire proof

cascade_argument.description: Solves the recursion via cascading induction. Lem 4.2 establishes sequence properties, Lem 4.3 initializes geometric decay on a subsequence, Lem 4.4 shows density of unit steps, Lem 4.5 bootstraps to every index

local_theory.description: Standard local well-posedness in $V_m$ with blowup criterion. Provides the initial solution that the global argument extends
